skill.name: openspec-daem0n-bridge
skill.description: Connects OpenSpec workflows with Daem0n-MCP memory by importing specs, recalling past outcomes, and recording archived changes as learnings.
skill.summary: Bridges OpenSpec specifications, proposal context, and archived change outcomes with Daem0n-MCP memory.
skill.author: DasBluEyedDevil

security_audit.summary: Static analysis flagged many shell and weak-cryptography patterns, but the weak-cryptography detections are false positives from prose and placeholders. The meaningful risks are automatic local file reads and persistence of OpenSpec content into Daem0n-MCP memory, which are legitimate for the skill but require clear user consent and path discipline.

medium_findings.title: Automatic Specification Import Into Persistent Memory
medium_findings.description: The skill instructs the assistant to detect OpenSpec directories automatically, read project specifications, and write extracted content and rules into Daem0n-MCP memory. This is aligned with the stated purpose, but it can persist proprietary requirements or sensitive design details without an explicit confirmation step.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The workflow explicitly combines automatic detection with memory writes. The risk is semantic data persistence, not hidden malicious behavior.

medium_findings.title: Local Shell Reads Depend On Project Path Inputs
medium_findings.description: The skill uses shell examples to list OpenSpec directories and read spec or archive files with placeholder names. This is useful for OpenSpec workflows, but implementations should constrain paths to expected OpenSpec directories and avoid accepting unchecked traversal input.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The commands are documented workflow examples rather than executable bundled scripts. The placeholders still create a realistic misuse risk if an agent substitutes untrusted values.

low_findings.title: Device File Access Is Benign Error Suppression
low_findings.description: The static filesystem finding points to redirecting ls errors to /dev/null while checking for an OpenSpec directory. This is a common local shell pattern and does not show data exfiltration or suspicious device access.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The only device path is /dev/null in a stderr redirect. No sensitive file, block device, or special device read is present.

low_findings.title: Weak Cryptography Detections Are Text False Positives
low_findings.description: The high static findings are caused by words and placeholders in Markdown, such as Daem0n-MCP references, rationale labels, and example descriptions. No hashing, encryption, password storage, or cryptographic implementation is present in the reviewed file.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: Manual review found Markdown instructions and examples only. There is no crypto API call or weak algorithm usage to confirm.

low_findings.title: System Reconnaissance Finding Is Benign Directory Discovery
low_findings.description: The reconnaissance finding maps to checking whether expected OpenSpec directories exist. This supports the skill workflow and does not enumerate host secrets, users, processes, networks, or environment variables.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The cited context is a change_id placeholder in memory metadata, not a host reconnaissance command. Nearby shell discovery is limited to project OpenSpec paths.

dangerous_patterns.title: Shell Commands For Project File Discovery
dangerous_patterns.description: The skill includes bash examples using ls and cat to inspect OpenSpec directories and files. These are legitimate for the integration, but agents should run them only inside the user workspace and with reviewed path values.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The external command pattern is real, but the commands are simple local reads rather than obfuscated execution or network activity.

dangerous_patterns.title: Persistent Memory Writes From Local Specifications
dangerous_patterns.description: The skill directs the assistant to save spec summaries, rules, proposal decisions, outcomes, and learnings to Daem0n-MCP. This can retain sensitive project information beyond the current session.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The memory-write behavior is explicitly documented in multiple workflows. The risk depends on the sensitivity of project specs and the Daem0n-MCP storage model.

content.user_title: Bridge OpenSpec Changes With Daem0n Memory
content.value_statement: OpenSpec projects can lose proposal context and past implementation lessons across sessions. This skill connects specs, proposals, and archived outcomes to Daem0n-MCP memory for repeatable development decisions.
actual_capabilities: Detects OpenSpec project directories before running import workflows.
Reads OpenSpec spec files and extracts requirement language into memory categories.
Creates Daem0n-MCP rules from MUST and MUST NOT requirements.
Recalls related patterns, warnings, decisions, and rules before proposal creation.
Records archived OpenSpec changes as outcomes and learnings.
Links proposal decisions to later implementation learnings.
limitations: Requires available Daem0n-MCP memory tools in the active environment.
Assumes a conventional OpenSpec directory layout under the project root.
May persist sensitive specification content if used without review.
Provides workflow instructions, not a standalone executable bridge.

use_cases.title: Keep Spec Rules Available During Implementation
use_cases.description: Import OpenSpec requirements into memory so the assistant can recall constraints before changing related code.

use_cases.title: Prepare Safer Change Proposals
use_cases.description: Review past decisions, warnings, and applicable rules before drafting a new OpenSpec proposal.

use_cases.title: Turn Archived Changes Into Team Learning
use_cases.description: Record completed proposal outcomes and link them to lessons that can guide future work.

prompt_templates.title: Import OpenSpec Specs
prompt_templates.scenario: Beginner setup for an existing OpenSpec project.
prompt_templates.prompt: Sync my OpenSpec specs to Daem0n memory. Show me what will be read before saving anything.

prompt_templates.title: Check Proposal Context
prompt_templates.scenario: Review memory before starting a new proposal.
prompt_templates.prompt: Prepare proposal context for adding [feature]. Recall related OpenSpec specs, warnings, past decisions, and rules.

prompt_templates.title: Record Archived Change Outcome
prompt_templates.scenario: Capture lessons after a completed OpenSpec change.
prompt_templates.prompt: I archived change [change-id]. Read the archive, find the original decision memory, and record the outcome and learnings.

prompt_templates.title: Audit Spec Memory Coverage
prompt_templates.scenario: Advanced review of whether memory matches current specs.
prompt_templates.prompt: Compare current OpenSpec specs with Daem0n memories. Identify stale, missing, or superseded rules before updating memory.

output_examples.input: Prepare proposal context for OAuth support.
output_examples.output: The assistant lists relevant authentication specs, prior warnings, applicable rules, and past decisions before asking whether to record the proposal intent.

output_examples.input: Record outcome for add-oauth-support after archive.
output_examples.output: The assistant summarizes the archived change, marks the proposal decision as completed, creates implementation learnings, and links them to the original decision.

output_examples.input: Refresh OpenSpec memory after requirement changes.
output_examples.output: The assistant reviews current spec files, identifies changed requirements, and proposes memory updates for approval.

best_practices: Review spec content before allowing it to be stored in persistent memory.
Keep OpenSpec change IDs and directory names constrained to expected project paths.
Refresh memories after archived changes so future proposals use current requirements.
anti_patterns: Do not import private or regulated specs into shared memory without approval.
Do not run file reads outside the project workspace.
Do not rely on stale memories when the OpenSpec files have changed.

faq.question: Does this skill execute a background service?
faq.answer: No. It provides workflow instructions for an assistant using OpenSpec files and Daem0n-MCP memory tools.

faq.question: Does it require Daem0n-MCP?
faq.answer: Yes. The main workflows depend on Daem0n-MCP memory, rule, recall, and outcome tools.

faq.question: Can it work without OpenSpec?
faq.answer: No. The workflows assume OpenSpec specs and archived changes exist in the project.

faq.question: What should I review before importing specs?
faq.answer: Review whether the specs contain proprietary, customer, security, or regulated information before saving them to memory.

faq.question: Why is the security risk medium?
faq.answer: The skill uses local file reads and persistent memory writes. They are expected features, but they need user oversight.

faq.question: Can Claude, Codex, and Claude Code use it?
faq.answer: The report marks support for Claude, Codex, and Claude Code, assuming the needed MCP tools are available.
